documents.title: Architecture overview
documents.text:
• Optimized transaction flow for coherent writes with a producer-consumer ordering model.
• Error reporting and propagation across components and interconnect for system reliability and integrity.
• Handling sub cache line Data Errors using Data Poisoning and per byte error indication.
• Power-aware signaling on the component interface:
– Enabling flit-level clock gating.
– Component activation and deactivation sequence for clock-gate and power-gate control.
– Protocol activity indication for power and clock control.

B1.1.3 Architecture layers

Functionality is grouped into the following layers:
• Protocol
• Network
• Link
Table B1.1 describes the primary function of each layer.

Table B1.1: Layers of the CHI architecture

Layer Communication granularity Primary function

Protocol Transaction
The Protocol layer is the topmost layer in the CHI architecture. The
function of the Protocol layer is to:
– Generate and process requests and responses at the protocol
nodes.
– Define the permitted cache state transitions at the protocol
nodes that include caches.
– Define the transaction flows for each request type.
– Manage the protocol level flow control.

Network Packet The function of the Network layer is to:
– Packetize the protocol message.
– Determine the source and target node IDs required to route
packet over the interconnect to the required destination and
to the packet.
– Determine the source and target node IDs required to route the
packet over the interconnect to the required destination and add

Link Flit
The
function of the Link layer is to:
– Provide flow control between network devices.
– Manage link channels to provide deadlock-free switching
across the network.

documents.title: Terminology
documents.text:
Chapter B1. Introduction
B1.3. Terminology

PoDP

Point of Persistence(PoP) is a potential point in a memory system at or beyond the Point of Coherency,
where a write to memory is maintained when system power is removed, and reliably recovered when
power is restored to the affected locations in memory.

(PoDP) is a point in the memory system where the data is preserved even
Point of Deep Persistence
when the power and the back up battery fail simultaneously.
PoPA
(PoPA) is a point where updates to a location in one Physical Address Space
Point of Physical Aliasing
(PAS) are visible to all other Physical Address Spaces.
PoPS
(PoPS) is a point furthest in the memory hierarchy away from a PE or another
Point of Physical Storage
observer to which a write transaction could propagate, that is, memory.
Downstream cache
A downstream cache is defined from the perspective of a Request Node. A downstream cache for a

Requester

Request is a cache that the Request accesses using CHI request transactions. A Request Node can send
a request with data to allocate data into a downstream cache.

A component that starts a transaction by issuing a request message. The term Requester can be used for
a component that independently initiates transactions. The term Requester can also be used for an

Completer

interconnect component that issues a downstream Request message independently or as a side-effect of
other transactions that are occurring in the system.

Any component that responds to a received transaction from another component. A Completer can

Subordinate

either be an interconnect component, such as a Home Node or a Miscellaneous Node, or a component,
such as a Subordinate, that is outside of the interconnect.

An agent that receives transactions and completes them appropriately. Typically, a Subordinate is the

Endpoint
most downstream agent in a system. A Subordinate can also be referred to as a Completer or Endpoint.

Protocol
Another name for a Subordinate component. An Endpoint is the final destination for a transaction.
Credit

Link
A credit, or guarantee, from a Completer that it will accept a transaction.
L-Credit
layer Credit,
A credit, or guarantee, that a flit will be accepted on the other side of the link. An L-Credit is a credit

ICN
for a single hop at the Link layer.

Interconnect (ICN) is the CHI transport mechanism that is used for communication between protocol
nodes. An interconnect can include an IMPLEMENTATION SPECIFIC fabric of switches connected in a
ring, mesh, crossbar, or another topology. The interconnect can also include protocol nodes, such as

IPA
Home Node and Miscellaneous Node.

Intermediate Physical Address(IPA). In two-stage address translation:

ARM IHI 0050

Issue H

• Stage 1 provides an Intermediate Physical Address (IPA).
• Stage 2 provides the Physical Address (PA).
RN
(RN) generates protocol transactions, including reads and writes, to the interconnect.
Request Node

(HN) is a node within the interconnect that receives protocol transactions from Request
HN
Home Node
Nodes, completes the required coherency action, and returns a response.
SN
Subordinate Node (SN) is a node that receives a Request from a Home Node, completes the required
action, and returns a response.

node

MN
Miscellaneous or Misc Node (MN) is a node located within the interconnect that receives DVM
messages from Request Nodes, completes the required action, and returns a response.
IO Coherent
A Request Node that generates a subset of Snoopable requests in addition to Non-snoopable requests.
The Snoopable requests that an IO Coherent node generates do not result in the caching of the received
data in a coherent state. Therefore, an IO Coherent node does not receive any Snoop requests.

protocol

Snoopee
A Request Node that is receiving a snoop.
Write-Invalidate
A protocol in which a Request Node writing to a shared cache line in the system must invalidate all
copies before proceeding with the write. The CHI protocol is a Write-Invalidate protocol.
manner
In a timely
The protocol cannot define an absolute time within which something must occur. A sufficiently idle
system can progress and complete without explicit action.
Inapplicable
A field value that indicates that the field is not used in the processing of the message.

documents.title: Coherence overview
documents.text:
Valid, Invalid

EmptyA

When Valid, the cache line is present in the cache.
When Invalid, the cache line is not present in the cache.
Unique, Shared When Unique, the cache line exists only in the Unique cache.
When Shared, the cache line can exist in more than one cache. The cache line is not
guaranteed to exist in more than once cache.
Clean, Dirty When Clean, the cache does not have responsibility for updating main memory.
When Dirty, the cache line has been modified with respect to main memory. The Dirty
cache must ensure that main memory is eventually updated.
Full, Partial, Full cache line has all bytes valid.
A Partial cache line can have some bytes valid, where some include none or all bytes.
An Empty cache line has no bytes valid.
Figure B1.3 shows the seven state cache model. B4.1 Cache line statesgives further information about each cache
state.
A valid cache state name that is not Partial or Empty is considered to be Full. In Figure B1.3 , UC, UD, SC, and SD
are all Full cache line states.

Valid Invalid

Unique Shared

UC SC
Clean
UCE
I
UD SD
Dirty
UDP

Figure B1.3: Cache state model

documents.title: Memory Encryption Contexts, MEC
documents.text:
Chapter B10. Realm
B10.6. Memory

• DVMOp
The MECID field is inapplicable and can take any value in:
• Evict
• CleanShared
• CleanSharedPersist
• CleanSharedPersistSep
• CleanInvalid
• CleanInvalidPoPA
• CleanInvalidStorage
• MakeInvalid
• CleanUnique
• MakeUnique
The MECID field is applicable in all other requests, and can take any value bounded by the PAS relationship in
Table B13.47 .
B10.6.1.3 DAT channel
The MECID field is required on the DAT channel to remove the need for the snoop filter to be MECID aware. This
allows back invalidating snoops to be issued without knowledge of the MECID value in the Requester.
The MECID field shares a common field on the DAT channel with the DBID field.
The common field is considered as:
• MECID when DataPull is 0 and the message is SnpRespData, SnpRespDataPtl, or SnpRespDataFwded.
MECID can take any value bounded by the PAS relationship in Table B13.47 .
• DBID when DataPull is 1, or in any data message that is not SnpRespData, SnpRespDataPtl, or
SnpRespDataFwded.
The MECID field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other DAT messages.
If all bytes of a DAT message are marked with Poison or DERR, the MECID field is considered as poisoned.
B10.6.1.4 SNP channel
A Snoopee must be able to detect and act upon a MECID mismatch to a snooped Non-secure Protected or System
Agent location. See B10.8.1 MECID mismatch resolution .
A Snoopee is permitted, but not required, to detect and act upon a MECID mismatch to a snooped Realm location,
as determined by the MECID_Mismatch_Resolution_Realm property.
When a Snoopee requests DataPull as part of a Stash snoop response, it is avoiding any local translation
assignment or context assignment which would have otherwise assigned a MECID value. When allocating any
returned CompData, the MECID value used by the DataPull request is obtained from the snoop request.
The following applicability is required for the MECID field:
• Inapplicable and must be zero in SnpDVMOp and SnpQuery.
• Inapplicable and can take any value in SnpLCrdReturn.
• Applicable in all other Snoop messages and can take any value bounded by the PAS relationship in Table
B13.47.

ARM IHI 0050

B10.6.2 Memory accesses

If the interconnect forwards a request onto a Subordinate, it is expected to forward the MECID value along with
the request.
It is expected that if the interconnect caches a line, the MECID value is cached as well.

B10.6.3

Memory accesses that are the result of cache evictions, dedicated Cache Maintenance Operations, or snoop filter
back invalidations must use the MECID value that the entry was cached with, instead of the MECID value in any
associated request.

Stash transactions

When Home receives a Stash request and generates a Stash snoop to a Requester, the MECID value from the

B10.6.4

request must be sent along with the snoop.

Operations
Cache Maintenance

®
Architecture Reference Manual for A-profile architecture
An access to a cacheable memory location is associated with a MECID until reaching the PoE. The MEC
Architecture in Arm
states that Cache Maintenance
Operations to the Point of Physical Aliasing (PoPA) can affect all caches before the PoE.
Data is encrypted before being written to external memory or to any shared cache that resides past the PoPA.
Cache Maintenance Operations to the PoPA are sufficient to affect all caches before the PoE, and dedicated PoE
Cache Maintenance Operations are not required.
Memory accesses resulting from a cache Clean operation use the MECID that the entry was cached with.
In an RME system with MEC, the following Cache Maintenance Operations act, regardless of any MECID value
mismatches:
• CleanShared

B10.6.5
and Poison signaling

• CleanSharedPersist (+ Deep)
• CleanSharedPersistSep (+ Deep)
• CleanInvalid
• CleanInvalidStorage
• MakeInvalid
• CleanInvalidPoPA
Combined Write requests are issued with a MECID as per the requirement for an independent Write transaction.

MECID correctness

MECID correctness is required to be maintained to prevent data corruption. The MECID field metadata must be
propagated or corrected, as required, to ensure integrity across all transaction types, including speculative,
forwarded, and coherent operations.
If a cache implementation determines that a stored MECID is corrupt, any associated Data transferred from that
line is required to have the all the Poison field bits set or the RespErr field set to DERR.

Note
If Data is transferred without Poison or DERR, this may result in a silent corruption when the cache line is
later written back beyond the PoE using an incorrect encryption context.

Any Read transaction that derives its MECID value through address translation will correct any corrupt MECID
value at the point of allocation. However, the returned Data may be signaled as corrupt through the Poison field or

DERR response, if the Data was marked as corrupt in the Completer cache. Operations that clear the Poison field
from a cache line must ensure that the MECID associated with that line is updated to a known, suitable value.

Note
If the cache line is updated but the stored MECID field is not, any subsequent writeback beyond the PoE
may result in silent data corruption due to encryption with an incorrect context.

When a MECID field is provided as part of a Stash operation, either through a Write with Stash hint or through an
independent Stash request, the MECID field is required to always be considered correct. The same applies to
Immediate Writes that are not the result of a cache eviction. These transactions are assumed to originate from valid
architectural contexts and do not depend on cached metadata for MECID field correctness.

Note
Writes with Stash hint, independent Stash transactions, and Immediate Writes that are not derived from
cache state have a short lifetime or are directly issued by a Requester with a valid architectural context. As a
result, Writes with Stash hint, independent Stash transactions, and Immediate Writes are considered less
susceptible to poisoning than Data that resides within a cache structure for an extended period.
Consequently, the MECID correctness is assumed for such transactions.

Atomic* and PrefetchTgt transactions are required to treat the MECID field as correct in all cases.
Dataless write operations cannot convey the MECID field correctness information and are required to treat the
MECID field as correct. If a corrupted MECID field is detected prior to issuing a Dataless request, the
implementation is required to use a data-carrying version of the Write.

Chapter B10. Realm
B10.6. Memory

• DVMOp
The MECID field is inapplicable and can take any value in:
• Evict
• CleanShared

The MECID field is applicable in all other requests, and can take any value bounded by the PAS relationship in
Table B13.47 .
B10.6.1.3 DAT channel
The MECID field is required on the DAT channel to remove the need for the snoop filter to be MECID aware. This
allows back invalidating snoops to be issued without knowledge of the MECID value in the Requester.
The MECID field shares a common field on the DAT channel with the DBID field.
The common field is considered as:
• MECID when DataPull is 0 and the message is SnpRespData, SnpRespDataPtl, or SnpRespDataFwded.
MECID can take any value bounded by the PAS relationship in Table B13.47 .
• DBID when DataPull is 1, or in any data message that is not SnpRespData, SnpRespDataPtl, or
SnpRespDataFwded.
The MECID fiel

nship in Table B13.47 .
• DBID when DataPull is 1, or in any data message that is not SnpRespData, SnpRespDataPtl, or
SnpRespDataFwded.
The MECID field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other DAT messages.
If all bytes of a DAT message are marked with Poison or DERR, the MECID field is considered as poisoned.
B10.6.1.4 SNP channel
A Snoopee must be able to detect and act upon a MECID mismatch to a snooped Non-secure Protected or System
Agent location. See B10.8.1 MECID mismatch resolution .
A Snoopee is permitted, but not required, to detect and act upon a MECID mismatch to a snooped Realm location,
as determined by the MECID_Mismatch_Resolution_Realm property.
When a Snoopee requests DataPull as part of a Stash snoop response, it is avoiding any local translation
assignment or context assignment which would have otherwise assigned a MECID value. When allocating any
returned CompData, the MECID value used by the DataPull request is obtained from the snoop request.

• Inapplicable and can take any value in SnpLCrdReturn.
• Applicable in all other Snoop messages and can take any value bounded by the PAS relationship in Table
B13.47.

documents.title: Device Assignment (DA) and Coherent Device Assignment (CDA)
documents.full_title: B10.7 Device Assignment (DA) and Coherent Device Assignment (CDA)
documents.text:
A device is a single physical chip(let) and is treated as a single unit from a trust perspective and individually
attested.
A device:

(DCM) directly attached to it. The DCM can be

• Is trusted in its entirety for a set of Realms only and trusted to maintain Realm separation where required.
• Can have private memory-mapped resources.
• Can have one or more interfaces.
• Can have memory termed as Device Coherent Memory
coherently accessed by the host, the device, and by other coherent devices.
• Can access the DCM of another coherent device only where that access is channeled through a host compute
node.
• Only operates in the Realm and Non-secure PAS.
• Is expected, but not required, to include the following fields in all requests to the host:
– SecSID1
– StreamID
• Only receives snoop requests for:
– Regions of memory owned by Realms that trust the device
– Non-secure regions of memory

®
Architecture

• Cannot be directly connected to another device.
• Is not permitted to send DVMOp requests or SnpDVMOp snoops towards to a host.
The host and the devices can have multiple Request Nodes and Home Nodes.
It is expected that the host will perform additional checks on certain messages from or to a device. These checks
ensure that:
• Requests coming from the device to HCM are permitted to access that memory. This means the device
cannot issue a request with a random PA and expect to get access to that location.
• Snoops coming from the device are only for DCM. This means the device cannot issue a request with
arbitrary PA to gain access to a location.
• Snoops going to the device for cached HCM locations only expose accesses that the device is permitted to
observe. This means the device cannot build up a picture of all trusted system activity for more targeted
attacks.
For more information on RME-DA and RME-CDA, see Arm
Reference Manual for A-profile
architecture.

B10.7.2 Field applicability

Support for RME-DA or RME-CDA at an interface is determined by the DevAssign_Support property. The
property defines if the SecSID1 and StreamID fields are present on that interface.
The applicability of StreamID and SecSID1 fields in relation to the various messages is detailed in this section:
• B10.7.2.1 Device request to HCM location or DCM location of a peer device
• B10.7.2.2 Host request to DCM location
• B10.7.2.3 Device snoop to cached DCM location
• B10.7.2.4 Host snoop to cached HCM location or cached DCM location of a peer device
The omission of a field name indicates that the related field is not applicable in the associated message.

Chapter B10. Realm
B10.8. Granular

Data must be the cached data.
*
MECID must be the cached MECID value.
*
MismatchedMECID must be set to 1.
*
– No change in permitted state transitions or responses for SnpCleanShared:
If providing SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl:
*
· Data must be the cached data.

. If SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl is provided:

· MECID must be the cached MECID .
· MismatchedMECID must be 1.
– No change in behavior required for SnpQuery or SnpMakeInvalid.
• For Stashing snoops:
– DataPull is not permitted.
– No change in behavior required for SnpMakeInvalidStash, SnpStashUnique, and SnpStashShared.
– SnpUniqueStash permits SnpRespData, SnpRespDataPtl, or SnpResp_I, depending on start state and
RetToSrc

Data must be the cached data.

*

for a Home
*
MismatchedMECID must be set to 1.
B10.8.1.3 Requirements
The requirements listed in this section apply to transactions that target a:
• System Agent or Non-secure Protected PAS, when the GDI_Support is True.
• Realm PAS, when the MECID_Mismatch_Resolution_Realm property is True.

IMPLEMENTATIONto an

When Home receives MismatchedMECID set to 1 in SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl, it must establish whether
there is any Data-masking required. Any action is dependent upon the original motivation for the snoop, which
includes:
• An incoming read request.
– Any CompData or DataSepResp that Home provides must be masked
value. It is recommended, but not required, that this value is all ones.
SPECIFIC
– Any SLC allocation or downstream write must use the Data and MECID values from the returned
SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl.

write allocates into the SLC – SnpRespData and SnpRespDataPtl must be
• An incoming partial write request.
If the partial
masked to anIMPLEMENTATION SPECIFIC value.
It is recommended, but not required, that this
value is all ones.
– SnpRespDataPtl must be treated as if all Byte
Enables were set to 1.
– The partial NonCopyBackWriteData must be
merged into this masked value.
– The MECID value of the cached location must be
the MECID of the incoming write request.
If the partial write does not allocate into the SLC and is processed locally

ARM IHI 0050

– SnpRespData and SnpRespDataPtl must be
masked to anIMPLEMENTATION SPECIFIC value.
It is recommended, but is not required, that this
value is all ones.
– SnpRespDataPtl must be treated as if all Byte
Enables were set to 1.
– The partial NonCopyBackWriteData must be
merged into this masked value.
– The downstream WriteNoSnpFull must use the
resulting Data value and the MECID of the
incoming write request.
If the partial write does not allocate into the SLC and is not processed locally
– Data must be written back to memory using the
Data and MECID values provided in
SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl.
– The downstream WriteNoSnpPtl must be ordered
behind the above write and must occur with the
Data and MECID values from the incoming
partial write.
• An incoming full line write request.
– No action is required. Any SLC allocation or downstream write must use the Data value and the
MECID of the incoming write request.
• An incoming StashOnce .
– Any SLC allocation of the returned Data must use the Data and MECID values provided in
SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl.
– Any CompData or DataSepResp that Home provides to a Stash target, which is different to the Snoopee
returning SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl, must be masked toIMPLEMENTATION an
SPECIFIC value.
It is recommended, but not required that this value is all ones.
• An incoming CMO request.
– No action is required. CMO transactions do not provide a MECID , therefore Home will be unable to
provide an accurate MECID on a snoop. Any write to memory must use the Data and MECID values
provided in SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl.
• A snoop filter back invalidation.
– No action is required. Snoop filters are not required to store the MECID of a location being tracked, so
will be unable to provide an accurate MECID on a snoop. Any write to memory must use the Data and
MECID values provided in SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl.

Chapter B11
System Control, Debug, Trace, and Monitoring

This chapter describes mechanisms that provide extra support for the controlling, debugging and tracing of
systems, and the monitoring of systems to enhance performance. It contains the following sections:
• B11.1 Quality of Service (QoS) mechanism
• B11.2 Data Source
• B11.3 Data Target
• B11.4 MPAM
• B11.5 Page-based Hardware Attributes
• B11.6 Completer Busy
• B11.7 Trace Tag

documents.title: Data Source
documents.text:
The CompleterType subfield encoding additionally determines the encoding space used for the Functional subfield.
The CompleterType subfield must correctly define the associated source, or use the Default encoding. A cache
must not use one of the memory groups, and a memory must not use one of the cache groups.
Cache Snoop Hit must be used for Snoopee caches that provide the data as a result of a CHI Snoop hit.
Any caches that are not covered by this must be grouped into Cache Group 1 or Cache Group 2.
The association of cache or memory types into the remaining groupings IMPLEMENTATION are
DEFINED .
The CompleterType subfield encodings for memory are shown in Table B11.2 .

Table B11.2: DataSource CompleterType encoding

DataSource[4:2] Architected Recommendation
CompleterType
0b000
Default Default

0b001

0b011

hit (Architected)

interconnect cache

interconnect cache

0b111
Reserved Reserved

B11.2.4 HitD

The DataSource [5] bit is used to transport Hitthe Dirty (HitD) subfield.
If the CompleterType indicates one of the cache encodings, the HitD subfield indicates if the cache line was in a
Dirty state when the cache lookup hit.

Note
Previously, a Requester issuing a transaction such as ReadNotSharedDirty may have seen Shared Clean data
returned, despite the line potentially being in a Unique Dirty state at the Snoopee.
The presence of the information in the HitD subfield helps provide more information to the Requester, which
may be useful for performance analysis.

The HitD encodings are shown in Table B11.3 .

Table B11.3: DataSource HitD encoding

DataSource[5] Description
HitD

0b0
Line was not in a Dirty state at the Completer

Continued on next page

Table B11.3 –Continued from previous page

DataSource[5] Description
HitD

0b1
Line was in a Dirty state at the Completer

The HitD subfield is applicable and can be set to 0 or 1 when the CompleterType is one of the cache encodings.
The HitD subfield must be 1 when the CompleterType is one of the cache encodings and the Data response
indicates pass Dirty [_PD].
The HitD subfield is inapplicable and must be zero when the CompleterType is not one of the cache encodings.

B11.2.5 Functional

The DataSource [7:6] bits are used to transport the Functional subfield.
The Functional subfield provides additional information that relates to the CompleterType selection.
A Completer does not need to support all of the Functional encodings for its CompleterType .
There are three types of Functional encodings:
• B11.2.5.1 Default
• B11.2.5.2 Memory
• B11.2.5.3 Cache
B11.2.5.1 Default
When the CompleterType is Default, the encoding of the Functional subfield is shown in Table B11.4 .

There are several reasons why the PrefetchTgt request might not be useful, including:
• There was no PrefetchTgt transaction before the actual request.
• The PrefetchTgt was dropped by the Subordinate.
• The data obtained by the PrefetchTgt was replaced in the buffer.
• The Read request arrived at the Subordinate before the PrefetchTgt .

B11.2. Data

B11.2.7 Driving and modifying DataSource[7:0]

This section describes driving and modifying the DataSource [7:0] flit.
value
B11.2.7.1 Initial
It is expected that the Completer, driving DataSource when applicable in responses, will set the
CompleterDistance value as if it were communicating to the closest possible Completer Distance.
For example, in Figure B11.1 , this means:
0b00 to,
• Processor 0 within any of the clusters should drive CompleterDistance in any applicable responses
Local cluster.
• Processor X should drive CompleterDistance in any applicable responses 0b01 to , Local die, because it
exists outside of any Local cluster configuration.
All Completers must drive DataSource [7:2] to a legal encoding.
It is recommended, but not required, that when an error is detected, the DataSource value is still driven accurately.
A Completer is permitted to select one of the encodings marked as Default where they exist, instead of one of the

modification

more defined encodings.
B11.2.7.2 Value
As the DataSource [7:0] value in any applicable response is transported through the system, it is expected that the
CompleterDistance is incremented as the appropriate boundary is crossed.

Note
IMPLEMENTATION
How the CompleterDistance value is modified as packets move through the system is
DEFINED . The modification can include a mixture of pass-through, increment by a fixed value, and set to a
specific value.

0b01 to , Local die. This
For example, in Figure B11.1 , this means:
• Any applicable response crossing the cluster boundary will be incremented
increment is performed by the cluster.

0b11 to , Remote socket. This

• Any applicable response crossing the die or chiplet boundary in the local socket will be incremented to
0b10, Remote die or chiplet. This increment is performed by the die-to-die or chiplet-to-chiplet transmitter.
• Any applicable response crossing the socket boundary will be incremented

It is permitted, but not expected, that any changes are made to DataSource [7:2] value when any applicable
response is transported through the system.

data from two different sources

Home is expected, but not required, to forward the unchanged DataSource value on to the Requester from the
SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl response from a Snoopee, or CompData or DataSepResp response from a
Subordinate.
B11.2.7.3 Combining
There are several scenarios where Home has to combine data from multiple sources before sending the CompData
or DataSepResp response back to a Requester:
• A Snoopee has a line in UDP state and another Requester issues a Read for that location. Home is required to
make a memory access to obtain the rest of the line.

ARM IHI

– Home is expected to use the Cache Snoop Hit CompleterType encoding when providing the CompData
or DataSepResp response back to the Requester.

• When MTE is enabled and a Requester issues a Read to obtain data and tags, but the data is only present in a
Snoopee and not in the tags. Home is required to make a memory access to obtain the MTE tags.
– Home is expected to use the Cache Snoop Hit CompleterType encoding when providing the CompData

value for a cache encoding with both Late and Unused prefetch

or DataSepResp response back to the Requester.
• When Home has a copy of the line in the SLC, but requires access to memory to get the MTE tags.
– Home is expected to use the Cache Group 1 or Cache Group 2 CompleterType encoding when providing
the CompData or DataSepResp response back to the Requester.
B11.2.7.4 Functional
events
There is a scenario where Unused prefetch can be provided to Home in the SnpRespData response from a Snoopee,
whilst Home is also exposed to the Late prefetch event hazarding. In this scenario, Home is expected to use the
Late Prefetch Functional Cache encoding when providing the CompData response to the Requester.

The CompleterType subfield encoding additionally determines the encoding space used for the Functional subfield.
The CompleterType subfield must correctly define the associated source, or use the Default encoding. A cache
must not use one of the memory groups, and a memory must not use one of the cache groups.
Cache Snoop Hit must be used for Snoopee caches that provide the data as a result of a CHI Snoop hit.
Any caches that are not covered by this must be grouped into Cache Group 1 or Cache Group 2.
The association of cache or memory types into the remaining groupings IMPLEMENTATION are

B11.2.4 HitD

The DataSource [5] bit is used to transport Hitthe Dirty (HitD) subfield.
If the CompleterType indicates one of the cache encodings, the HitD subfield indicates if the cache line was in a
Dirty state when the cache lookup hit.

Note
Previously, a Requester issuing a transaction such as ReadNotSharedDirty may have seen Shared Clean data
returned, despite the line potentially being in a Unique Dirty state at the Snoopee.
The presence of the information in the HitD subfield helps provide more information to the Requester, which
may be useful for performance analysis.

The CacheLevel subfield can be used to indicate the optimal distance for the data being transferred by a Requester
to travel into the system cache hierarchy. This assists the intentional placement of data by a Producer, reducing the
access latency seen by a Consumer.
The CacheLevel subfield is applicable in:

• WriteUniqueFull

• ReadOnce

An implementation is permitted to ignore the hierarchy information indicated by the CacheLevel subfield value.
B11.3.2.4 Unique
The Unique subfield serves as an optional hint indicating that, in addition to allocating the data at a specific cache
level, the data should also be made Unique. The transition to a Unique state might require additional actions, such
as downstream transactions or additional snoops. A proactive state transition initiated by the Producer can help
reduce latency for a Consumer that intends to write to the same location by eliminating the need for
invalidation-based snoop transactions that might otherwise be required.
The Unique subfield is applicable in:
• When StashNIDValid is 0:
– StashOnceUnique
– StashOnceShared

Table B11.11: Unique subfield encodings

– StashOnceSepUnique
– StashOnceSepShared
• WriteEvictOrEvict
• WriteBackFull
• WriteCleanFull
When the CacheLevel subfield is 0, the Unique subfield must be 0.
When then CacheLevel subfield is non-zero, the Unique subfield can be 0 or 1.
The Unique subfield must be 0 in all other requests where the DataTarget is applicable.

An implementation is permitted to ignore the hierarchy information indicated by the CacheLevel subfield value.
B11.3.2.4 Unique
The Unique subfield serves as an optional hint indicating that, in addition to allocating the data at a specific cache
level, the data should also be made Unique. The transition to a Unique state might require additional actions, such
as downstream transactions or additional snoops. A proactive state transition initiated by the Producer can help
reduce latency for a Consumer that intends to write to the same location by eliminating the need for
invalidation-based snoop transactions that might otherwise be required.
The Unique subfield is applicable in:
• When StashNIDValid is 0:
– StashOnceUnique
– StashOnceShared

The Receiver is permitted, but not required, to support the full range of partitions and performance monitor groups

of the system are discovered and the range of partitions and the performance monitor groups used match the
capabilities of the system.

documents.title: Page-based Hardware Attributes
documents.text:
Note
A non-exhaustive list of how PBHA values can become imprecise include:
• Address translation is not used by the requests.
• PBHA value is not held in the caches.
• PBHA value is not provided alongside stashing transactions.
• Multiple Virtual Addresses mapped to the same PA where translations provide different PBHA values.
• Appropriate Cache Maintenance Operations have not been performed after the modification of the
PBHA value.

documents.title: Trace Tag
documents.text:
Examples, not an exhaustive list, of Request-Response pairs are:
• A Snoop response, with or without data, in response to a Snoop request.
• A Snoop response in response to a SnpDVMOp request.
• Data response from the Subordinate Node in response to a Read request.
• Spawned requests from HN-F:
– Snoops generated in response to a request from the Request Node.
– Request to SN-F generated in response to a request from the Request Node.
• Spawned request from HN-I:
– Read or Write request to SN-I generated in response to a request from the Request Node.
• A CompAck from the Request Node in response to CompData, Comp, or RespSepData.
• A RetryAck response from the Home Node or the Subordinate Node to any request.
• A ReadReceipt response from the Home Node or the Subordinate Node to a Read request or from the
Subordinate Node to a ReadNoSnpSep request.
• A DBIDResp response to a Write request.

RSP channel message:
• For a Comp response, the TagOp field is only used in response to a MakeReadUnique transaction and is used
to indicate if responsibility for Dirty tags is being passed to the Requester or not.

SNP message:
• The TagOp field is not present in the SNP channel.

Chapter B12. Memory
B12.4. Read

ReadPreferUnique with Transfer
*

Note

Transfer with ,

Prior to Issue G, the UC, UD, and UDP initial cache states at the sending of a
ReadPreferUnique were not permitted.
Issue G relaxes this constraint, allowing any start state for ReadPreferUnique
providing a more performant way to obtain MTE tags when the Requester has a Unique copy

· ReadClean with

ReadClean with

See Table B4.38 for Requester cache state transitions.
When responding to a ReadPreferUnique or ReadClean , a Home that uses a Snoop Filter to track the cached state
at the Requester, must not downgrade the state of the cache line in the Snoop Filter based on the state in the
response to the Requester. That is, the Snoop filter must not change a line previously tracked as:
• Valid, to being tracked as Invalid.
• Unique, to being tracked as Shared.

responses and tag state

• Dirty, to being tracked as Clean.

Note
Prior to Issue G, UC, UD, or UDP were not permitted initial cache line states for the ReadPreferUnique
transaction. The Home that used a Snoop Filter to track the cached states was permitted to set the state of the
cached line based on the state in the response.

B12.4.1.3 Permitted
The data and state of the cache line received with the Allocation Tags must be appropriately handled to not break
coherency.
Transferis , the permitted response field values are:
When the request TagOp value
• Transfer. Indicates the returned tags are Clean.
• Update. Indicates the returned tags are Dirty. Data response must pass Dirty [_PD].
Fetchis , the permitted response field values are:
When the request TagOp value
• Transfer. Indicates the returned tags are Clean.
• Update. Indicates the returned tags are Dirty. Data response must pass Dirty [_PD].
Invalidis , the permitted response field values are:
When the request TagOp value
• Invalid. Indicates the returned tags are Invalid.
• Transfer. Indicates the returned tags are Clean.
When the TagOp value in Read data is invalid, TU must be all zeros. Tag is inapplicable and can take any value.

ARM IHI 0050

When data and response are separately sent in a Read transaction, the TagOp field is only applicable in the
Data-only message. TagOp is inapplicable in the Non-data response message and must be set to 0.

The cache state that the tags must be held in is consistent with the type of the Read request:
• For all Read requests with a TagOp value Invalid of , Invalid or Clean tags must be returned.

Invalid of.

• For ReadNoSnp with a TagOp valueTransfer of or Fetch, Clean tags must be returned.
• For ReadClean , ReadOnce , ReadOnceCleanInvalid , and ReadOnceMakeInvalid with a TagOp value of
Transfer, Clean tags must be returned.
• For ReadNotSharedDirty with a TagOp valueTransfer of , Clean or Dirty tags must be returned. Dirty tags
are permitted to be returned only if the cache line state is Unique.
• For ReadShared with a TagOp valueTransfer of , Clean or Dirty tags must be returned.
• For ReadUnique with a TagOp value Transferof or Fetch, Clean or Dirty tags must be returned. The returned
cache line state must be Unique.
• For MakeReadUnique with a TagOp value Invalidof , Invalid or Clean tags must be returned. Clean tags are
only permitted in responses with data.
• For MakeReadUnique with a TagOp value Transferof , if data is included in the response, Clean or Dirty tags
must be returned.
– Clean tags are permitted in responses with data.
– Dirty tags are only permitted when the response is transferring the responsibility of updating Dirty data,
that is, the response includes [UD_PD].
– Dataless responses using Comp_UC or Comp_SC are permitted to signal Clean tags are being
transferred, despite no Tag transfer occurring.
• In the case where Dirty tags are returned, the cache line returned must include pass Dirty [_PD].
When MTE is not supported for the targeted address, the response must use TagOp
For an Exclusive access sequence, the fetching of tags must avoid any form of request that Invalidates other copies
of the cache line before the Exclusive Store transaction is performed. Typically, this is achieved by fetching tags at
the same time the Exclusive Load transaction is performed.

TagOp value in the corresponding request. The combination of tag and data states must obey the coherency rules
described in B12.3Tag coherency.

Data-only message. TagOp is inapplicable in the Non-data response message and must be set to 0.

The cache state that the tags must be held in is consistent with the type of the Read request:
• For all Read requests with a TagOp value Invalid of , Invalid or Clean tags must be returned.

• For ReadNotSharedDirty with a TagOp valueTransfer of , Clean or Dirty tags must be returned. Dirty tags
are permitted to be returned only if the cache line state is Unique.
• For ReadShared with a TagOp valueTransfer of , Clean or Dirty tags must be returned.
• For ReadUnique with a TagOp value Transferof or Fetch, Clean or Dirty tags must be returned. The returned
cache line state must be Unique.
• For MakeReadUnique with a TagOp value Invalidof , Invalid or Clean tags must be returned. Clean tags are
only permitted in responses with data.
• For MakeReadUnique with a TagOp value Transferof , if data is included in the response, Clean or Dirty tags

documents.title: Write transactions
documents.text:
Chapter B12. Memory
B12.5. Write

• For WriteBackPtl with TagOp :
– Transfer: Not permitted.

• For WriteNoSnpFull with TagOp :

– Update: Not permitted.
– Match: Not permitted.

– Transfer: TU bits are inapplicable and must be zero. Clean tag transfer is permitted from the Request
Node to the Home Node as well as the Home Node to the Subordinate Node.

• For WriteNoSnpDef with TagOp :

– Update: All TU bits must be 1.
– Match: TU bits are inapplicable and must be zero.

– Transfer: Not permitted.
– Update: Not permitted.

• For WriteUniqueFull and WriteUniqueFullStash with TagOp :
– Match: Not permitted.

– Transfer: Not permitted.
– Update: All TU bits must be 1.

• For WriteNoSnpPtl , WriteUniquePtl , and WriteUniquePtlStash with TagOp :
– Match: TU bits are inapplicable and must be zero.

– Transfer: Not permitted.
– Update: Any combination of TU and BE bits can be 1, including all ones or all zeros.

• For WriteEvictFull and WriteEvictOrEvict with TagOp :

– Match: TU bits are inapplicable and must be zero. Tag Match must be performed for only those tags
that have at least one corresponding BE = 1. A Tag Match must not be performed when all BE bits are
set to 0.

– Transfer: Clean tags must be returned. TU bits are inapplicable and must be zero.
– Update: Not permitted.

• For WriteNoSnpZero and WriteUniqueZero only TagOp
– Match: Not permitted.
Invalid is permitted.
For a Write request with a TagOpMatch of the tags within the size wrap boundary can take any value, and the tags
outside of size are inapplicable and can also take any value.
In the WriteDataCancel Write data response, irrespective of the TagOp value in the Write request, the MTE fields
are inapplicable and must be zero.
In Write data, with TagOp Invalid, all the TU bits and all the Tag bits must be zero.

documents.title: Snoop requests
documents.text:
• For SnpOnceFwd:
• Permitted to return Dirty tags to the Home.
• Dirty tags must be kept at the Snoopee if the data transfer to Home is not performed.
• Dirty tags must be written back to Home if the cache line is being invalidated or Dirty data is being written
back to Home.

TagOp values in Snoop responses for more details.

A Snoopee is permitted, but not required, to convert any Forwarding snoop to its corresponding Non-forwarding
snoop.

Note
This property is similar to that in the non-MTE case.

See B12.9.4 Permitted

B12.9.3 Stash snoops

Because the SNP channel does not include a TagOp field, the Home cannot forward the TagOp intentions of the
Requester to the Stash target.

, and in response to SnpUniqueStash.

The permitted TagOp values in Snoop responses are:
• Invalid in response to SnpStash*.
• Invalid, Transfer Update

TagOp values in Snoop responses for more details.

See B12.9.4 Permitted
The requirements for determining the TagOp value in the Read request implied from the Data Pull request in the
Snoop response are as follows:
In response to SnpStash*:
Transfer is , it is recommended that Clean tags are returned in
• If the TagOp value in the original request
response to the DataPull request.
• If the TagOp value in the original request Invalid is , it is recommended that Clean tags are returned in
response to the DataPull request if tags are available.
In response to SnpUniqueStash:
• If tags are available when data is returned, irrespective of the presence or absence of data in the Snoop
response and for any cache state, it is recommended that the Clean tags are returned in response to a DataPull

B12.9.4

request.

values in Snoop responses

In response to SnpMakeInvalidStash:
• If Clean tags are available, it is recommended that they are returned in response to a DataPull request.

Permitted TagOp

The permitted tag field values in Snoop responses are:
• For SnpResp, the TagOp field is inapplicable and must be zero.
• For SnpRespDataPtl, the permitted TagOp value is Invalid. Both the TU and the Tag field must be set to 0
and ignored by the receiver.
• For SnpRespData:
– Invalid. All Tag fields must be zero.

B13.10.1
QoS

Quality of Service,

The QoS field is used to assign a Quality of Service (QoS) value to a transaction. Ascending values of QoS

.
indicate higher priority levels.
See B11.1 Quality of Service (QoS) mechanism

B13.10.2 Target Identifier, TgtID

The TgtID field is the node ID of the component to which the message is targeted. This is used by the interconnect
to determine the port to which the message is sent.
.
See B2.4.1 Target Identifier, TgtID, and Source Identifier, SrcID

B13.10.3 Source Identifier, SrcID

The SrcID field is the node ID of the component from which the message is sent. This is used by the interconnect
to determine the port from which the message is sent.
.
See B2.4.1 Target Identifier, TgtID, and Source Identifier, SrcID

B13.10.4
HomeNID
Home Node Identifier,

The HomeNID field is associated with the original request. The Requester uses the value in this field to determine
the TgtID of the CompAck to be sent in response to CompData.
The HomeNID field is applicable in CompData and DataSepResp.

.
The HomeNID field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other Data messages.
See B2.4.11 Home Node Identifier, HomeNID

B13.10.5
ReturnNID
Return Node Identifier,

The ReturnNID field identifies the node to which the Subordinate sends a CompData, DataSepResp, or a Persist
response. This field also indicates the node to which the Subordinate sends DBIDResp when DoDWT = 1. The
value can be either the node ID of Home or the Requester that originated the transaction.

.

The ReturnNID field is applicable from Home to Subordinate in ReadNoSnp , ReadNoSnpSep ,
CleanSharedPersistSep, WriteNoSnp , WriteNoSnpDef , Combined Write, and Atomic requests.
The ReturnNID field is inapplicable and must be zero for all other requests. For Stash requests, the same bits in the
packet are used for StashNID .
See B2.4.10 Return Node Identifier, ReturnNID

B13.10.6 Forward Node Identifier, FwdNID

The FwdNID field identifies the Requester to which the CompData response can be forwarded. The value must be
the node ID of the Requester that initiated the transaction.
The FwdNID field is applicable in Forward-type snoops.
The FwdNID field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other Snoop requests, except range-based TLBI DVM

.

operations.
In range-based TLBI DVM operations, the bits in the field are used for DVM payload.
See B2.4.12 Forward Node Identifier, FwdNID

B13.10.7
Identifier, LPID
Logical Processor

The LPID field is used when a single Requester contains more than one logically separate processing agent. The
SrcID is used with LPID to uniquely identify the LP that generated the request.
The LPID field must be set to the correct value for the following transactions:
• For any Non-snoopable Non-cacheable or Device access:
– ReadNoSnp
– WriteNoSnp
– WriteNoSnpDef
• For Exclusive accesses, that can be one of the following transaction types:

for further details.

– ReadClean
– ReadShared
– ReadNotSharedDirty
– ReadPreferUnique
– MakeReadUnique
– CleanUnique
– ReadNoSnp
– WriteNoSnp
See Chapter B6Exclusive accesses
In requests, when applicable, the same bits in the packet are used for TagGroupID , PGroupID , and StashGroupID .
For other transactions, the LPID field value is permitted, but not required, to indicate the original LP that caused a

.
transaction to be issued.
See B2.4.7 Logical Processor Identifier, LPID

B13.10.8 Persistence Group Identifier, PGroupID

The PGroupID field is used by a Requester to identify and process different sets of CleanSharedPersistSep
transactions by grouping them together.
The PGroupID field is applicable in the CleanSharedPersistSep and Write*CleanShPerSep requests and the Persist
and CompPersist responses.
The PGroupID field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other requests and responses.
In requests, when applicable, the same bits in the packet are used for LPID , TagGroupID , and StashGroupID .
In responses, when applicable, the same bits in the packet are used for DBID , TagGroupID , and StashGroupID .

See B2.4.13 Persistence Group Identifier, PGroupID .

B13.10.9
Identifier, StashNID
Stash Node

The StashNID field identifies the target of the Stash request. The StashNID field provides a valid stash target value
when the corresponding StashNIDValid bit is 1.
The StashNID field is applicable in Stash requests.

.

The StashNID field is inapplicable and must be zero for all other requests.
For ReadNoSnp and ReadNoSnpSep requests, the same bits in the packet are used for ReturnNID .
See B7.4 Stash target identifiers

B13.10.10 Stash Node Identifier Valid, StashNIDValid

The StashNIDValid field indicates if the StashNID field has a valid value.
The StashNIDValid field is applicable in Stash requests.
The StashNIDValid field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other requests.

Table B13.10: StashNIDValid value encodings

Table B13.10 shows the StashNIDValid value encodings.

StashNID field value is inapplicable and must be zero

0b1

.

See B7.4 Stash target identifiers

B13.10.11 Stash Logical Processor Identifier, StashLPID

The StashLPID field provides a valid LP target value within the Request Node specified by StashNID .
The StashLPID field is applicable in Stash requests and Stash Snoop requests.
For ReadNoSnp requests, the same bits in the packet are used for ReturnTxnID .
The StashLPID field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other requests and snoop requests.

.

For Forwarding snoops, the same bits in the packet are used for FwdTxnID and for SnpDVMOp snoops the same
bits in the packet are used for VMIDExt .
See B7.4 Stash target identifiers

The StashLPIDValid field indicates if the StashLPID field has a valid value.
The StashLPIDValid field is applicable in Stash requests and Stash snoop requests.
The StashLPIDValid field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other requests and snoop requests.

B13.10.13 Group Identifier, StashGroupID
Stash

The StashGroupID field is used by a Requester to identify and process different sets of StashOnceSep transactions
by grouping them together.
The StashGroupID field is applicable only in the StashOnceSep request and StashDone response.

Stash Group Identifier, StashGroupID .

The StashGroupID field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other requests and responses.
In requests, when applicable, the same bits in the packet are used for LPID , TagGroupID , and PGroupID .
In responses, when applicable, the same bits in the packet are used for DBID , TagGroupID , and PGroupID .
See B2.4.14

B13.10.14 Transaction Identifier, TxnID

The TxnID field provides the transaction ID of the message. When there are multiple outstanding transactions
from a given source node they will each use a unique transaction ID.

Transaction Identifier, TxnID .
The TxnID in LCRdReturn must be zero.
See B2.4.2

B13.10.15 Return Transaction Identifier, ReturnTxnID

The ReturnTxnID field identifies the value that the Subordinate must use in the TxnID field of the CompData and
DataSepResp response.
The ReturnTxnID field can be either the TxnID generated by Home for this transaction or the TxnID in the
Request packet from the Requester that originated the transaction.
The ReturnTxnID field is applicable only in ReadNoSnp , ReadNoSnpSep , WriteNoSnp , WriteNoSnpDef ,

Return Transaction Identifier, ReturnTxnID

Combined Write, and Atomic requests from Home to Subordinate.
The ReturnTxnID field is inapplicable and must be zero for all other requests.
For Stash requests, the same bits in the packet are used for StashLPID .
See B2.4.4
.

B13.10.16 Forwarding Transaction Identifier, FwdTxnID

The FwdTxnID field identifies the TxnID field of the original request associated with the Snoop transaction.
The FwdTxnID field is applicable in Forward-type snoops.
The FwdTxnID field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other Snoop requests.
For Stash snoops, the same bits in the packet are used for StashLPID .
For SnpDVMOp snoops, the same bits in the packet are used for VMIDExt .

See B2.4.5 Forward Transaction Identifier, FwdTxnID .

B13.10.17
DBID
Data Buffer Identifier,

response
The DBID field is used for the TxnID field for CompAck or WriteData sent from the Requester in the
packet from a Completer.
Data Pull
In Snoop responses with Data Pull, the DBID value indicates the value to be used in the TxnID field of
response messages.

.

In responses, when applicable, the same bits in the packet are used for PGroupID , StashGroupID , and
TagGroupID.
See B2.4.3 Data Buffer Identifier, DBID

B13.10.19 Deep persistence, Deep

The Deep field is used by the Requester to indicate that writes must have been written to the final destination
before the Completer can provide certain responses.
The Deep field is applicable in the CleanSharedPersist* request and Combined Write request with
CleanSharedPersistSep.
The Deep field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other requests.
When the Deep field is 0:
• All earlier writes must have reached the PoP before the Completer can send:
– Comp for a CleanSharedPersist transaction
– Persist or CompPersist for a CleanSharedPersistSep transaction
• The PoP is the point at which it is guaranteed that sufficient time is available to make the data persistent after

B13.10.20

loss of power.
When the Deep field is 1:
• All earlier writes must have been written to the final destination, as well as the PoP, before the Completer can
send:
– Comp for a CleanSharedPersist transaction
– Persist or CompPersist for a CleanSharedPersistSep transaction
• The final destination is the point at which no time is required to make the data persistent after the loss of
power. If battery failure occurs, this ensures data preservation.
See Table B2.7 for more information on response ordering guarantees.

Address, Addr

The Addr field specifies the address associated with the message.
A PA of 44 to 52 bits is supported. The address is carried in Addr field in the REQ and SNP channels.
The width of the Addr field is defined by the Req_Addr_Width parameter. The Addr field width is the same value
of the parameter in REQ channel, Addr[(43-51):0], and is three less than the parameter value in the SNP channel,
Addr[(43-51):3].
The Addr field is utilized in the REQ and SNP channels in the following manner:

• For Read, PrefetchTgt , Dataless, Write, and Atomic* transactions, the Addr field includes the address of the
memory location being accessed. This starts with Addr[0] mapped to bit 0 of Addr.
• For a snoop request, except SnpDVMOp, the Addr field includes the address of the location being snooped.
This starts with Addr[3] mapped to bit 0 of Addr.

– Addr[(43-51):6] is the cache line address. It is sufficient to uniquely identify the cache line to be
accessed by the snoop.
Critical Chunk
– Addr[5:4] identifies the critical chunk being accessed by the transaction. See B2.9.7
. It is recommended that the snooped cache returns the data in wrap order with the critical
Identifier
chunk returned first.

Note

DVM Operations

Addr[3] in the REQ channel, that is Addr[0] in SNP channel, is supplied but is not used by a
snoop request.

• For a DVMOp and SnpDVMOp request, the Addr field is used to carry information related to a DVM
operation. See Chapter B8
.

.
• The Addr field value is not used for the PCrdReturn transaction and must be zero.
See B2.8.1 Address

B13.10.39 Tag

The Tag field provides n sets of 4-bit tags, Tag[4*n-1:0]. Each tag is associated with a 16-byte, aligned address
location.

Memory Tagging .
Tag[((4*n)-1) : 4*(n-1)] corresponds to Data [(128*n)-1 : 128*(n-1)].
For more information, see Chapter B12

B13.10.40 Tag Update, TU

The TU field indicates which of the Allocation Tags must be updated. There is one TU bit for each tag.
The TU field is applicable in Snoop responses and Write transactions which update the Allocation Tags.

The TU field must be zero in all other transactions.
TU[n-1] corresponds to Tag [(4*n)-1 : 4*(n-1)].
Memory Tagging .

B13.10.48 Completer Busy, CBusy

The CBusy field is a mechanism for the Completer of a transaction to indicate its current level of activity.
The CBusy field value encodings are IMPLEMENTATION DEFINED .
See B11.6 Completer Busy.

The Data field is the data payload that is being transported in a Data packet.
The following data bus widths are supported:
• 128-bit
• 256-bit
• 512-bit

Data source, DataSource

The DataSource field identifies the sender of the data response, and can provide additional information on the state
of the data in a system.
The DataSource field is applicable in:
• CompData and DataSepResp responses in Read and Atomic transactions
• SnpRespData and SnpRespDataPtl responses
The DataSource field is not applicable and must be zero in all other responses.

B13.10.56

Note
Prior to Issue G, the DataSource field was only applicable in SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl responses in
Non-stash type Snoop transactions. This requirement was driven by DataPull sharing the common field
location on the SNP channel. With DataPull being split out for Issue G, this restriction is removed, allowing
DataSource to be applicable in all SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl responses.

See B11.2 Data Source.

Data Target, DataTarget

The DataTarget field forwards replacement and usage hints from Requesters to the caches in the interconnect.

See B11.3 Data Target .

B13.10.62 Stream Identifier, StreamID

The StreamID field is used as a unique identifier of a stream of requests originating from one or a set of Requesters
associated with the same System MMU context.
The StreamID field is present when the DevAssign_Support property is set to Device_StreamID_SecSID1.
When interfacing to PCIe, the StreamID field is the Requester node ID associated with a single PCIe endpoint.
The StreamID field is applicable and can take any value in all device requests to the host, except for the following

Device Assignment (DA) and Coherent Device Assignment (CDA) .

requests when StreamID is inapplicable and must be zero:
• PCrdReturn
• DVMOp
The StreamID field not present in host requests to the device.
See B10.7

B13.10.63 Stream Identifier Security State, SecSID1

The SecSID1 field qualifies the Security state of the StreamID .
The SecSID1 field is present when the DevAssign_Support property is set to Device_StreamID_SecSID1.
The SecSID1 field is applicable and can take any value in all device requests to the host, except for the following
requests when SecSID1 is inapplicable and must be zero:
• PCrdReturn

Device Assignment (DA) and Coherent Device Assignment (CDA) .

• DVMOp
The SecSID1 field is not present in host requests to the device.
For the permitted encodings of SecSID1 against PAS, see Table B10.3 .

B13.10.64 MultiReq

The MultiReq field is used in conjunction with the NumReq field to indicate the total amount of Data associated
with the transaction.
The MultiReq field is applicable in:
• ReadNoSnp
• ReadNoSnpSep
• ReadOnce
• ReadOnceCleanInvalid
• ReadOnceMakeInvalid

• WriteNoSnpFull
• WriteNoSnpPtl
• WriteNoSnpZero
• WriteUniqueFull
• WriteUniquePtl
• WriteUniqueZero
The MultiReq field is inapplicable and can take any value in ReqLCrdReturn .
The MultiReq field is inapplicable and must be 0 in all other requests.
See B2.6 Multi-request for more information.

B13.10.65 NumReq

The NumReq field is used in conjunction with the MultiReq field to indicate the total amount of Data associated
with the transaction.
The least significant 3 bits of NumReq share a common field with the Size field.
When the MultiReq field is 0 The common field is considered Size .
When the MultiReq field is 1 The common field is considered NumReq.

B13.10.66 CacheLineID

The CacheLineID field indicates to which cache line a Response or Data message is related.
When applicable, the CacheLineID field must match the value of REQ. Addr[11:6] or SNP. Addr[8:3] for the
relevant line being accessed, for both multi-request and non multi-request transactions.
For example, a multi-request ReadNoSnp transaction targeting four cache lines starting at address location 0x0C0
would see CompData, or RespSepData or DataSepResp messages with the following CacheLineID values:
• 0b000011 indicating the cache line at address 0x0C0
• 0b000100 indicating the cache line at address 0x100
• 0b000101 indicating the cache line at address 0x140
• 0b000110 indicating the cache line at address 0x180
The CacheLineID field is applicable in the following Response messages, except for when they are part of a DVM
transaction:
• Comp
• RespSepData
• CompDBIDResp
• DBIDResp
• DBIDRespOrd
• ReadReceipt
• CompAck
The CacheLineID field is inapplicable and must be 0 in the following Response messages when it is part of a
DVM transaction:
• DBIDResp
• Comp
• CompDBIDResp
The CacheLineID field is applicable in the following data messages:
• CompData
• DataSepResp

B13.10.67

• SnpRespData
• SnpRespDataPtl
• SnpRespDataFwded
The CacheLineID field is inapplicable and can take any value in:
• RspLCrdReturn
• DatLCrdReturn
The CacheLineID field is inapplicable and must be 0 in all other Response and Data messages.
See B2.6 Multi-request for more information.

The HomeNID field is associated with the original request. The Requester uses the value in this field to determine
the TgtID of the CompAck to be sent in response to CompData.
The HomeNID field is applicable in CompData and DataSepResp.

.

Return Node Identifier,

The ReturnNID field identifies the node to which the Subordinate sends a CompData, DataSepResp, or a Persist
response. This field also indicates the node to which the Subordinate sends DBIDResp when DoDWT = 1. The
value can be either the node ID of Home or the Requester that originated the transaction.

.

The ReturnNID field is applicable from Home to Subordinate in ReadNoSnp , ReadNoSnpSep ,
CleanSharedPersistSep, WriteNoSnp , WriteNoSnpDef , Combined Write, and Atomic requests.
The ReturnNID field is inapplicable and must be zero for all other requests. For Stash requests, the same bits in the
packet are used for StashNID .

B13.10.6 Forward Node Identifier, FwdNID

The FwdNID field identifies the Requester to which the CompData response can be forwarded. The value must be
the node ID of the Requester that initiated the transaction.
The FwdNID field is applicable in Forward-type snoops.
The FwdNID field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other Snoop requests, except range-based TLBI DVM

.

operations.

B13.10.8 Persistence Group Identifier, PGroupID

The PGroupID field is used by a Requester to identify and process different sets of CleanSharedPersistSep
transactions by grouping them together.
The PGroupID field is applicable in the CleanSharedPersistSep and Write*CleanShPerSep requests and the Persist
and CompPersist responses.
The PGroupID field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other requests and responses.
In requests, when applicable, the same bits in the packet are used for LPID , TagGroupID , and StashGroupID .
In responses, when applicable, the same bits in the packet are used for DBID , TagGroupID , and StashGroupID .

See B2.4.13 Persistence Group Identifier, PGroupID .

B13.10.14 Transaction Identifier, TxnID

The TxnID field provides the transaction ID of the message. When there are multiple outstanding transactions
from a given source node they will each use a unique transaction ID.

Transaction Identifier, TxnID .

The ReturnTxnID field identifies the value that the Subordinate must use in the TxnID field of the CompData and
DataSepResp response.
The ReturnTxnID field can be either the TxnID generated by Home for this transaction or the TxnID in the
Request packet from the Requester that originated the transaction.

B13.10.16 Forwarding Transaction Identifier, FwdTxnID

The FwdTxnID field identifies the TxnID field of the original request associated with the Snoop transaction.

The FwdTxnID field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other Snoop requests.

B13.10.19 Deep persistence, Deep

The Deep field is used by the Requester to indicate that writes must have been written to the final destination
before the Completer can provide certain responses.
The Deep field is applicable in the CleanSharedPersist* request and Combined Write request with
CleanSharedPersistSep.
The Deep field is inapplicable and must be zero in all other requests.
When the Deep field is 0:
• All earlier writes must have reached the PoP before the Completer can send:
– Comp for a CleanSharedPersist transaction
– Persist or CompPersist for a CleanSharedPersistSep transaction
• The PoP is the point at which it is guaranteed that sufficient time is available to make the data persistent after

B13.10.20

PersistSep transaction
• The PoP is the point at which it is guaranteed that sufficient time is available to make the data persistent after

B13.10.20

loss of power.
When the Deep field is 1:
• All earlier writes must have been written to the final destination, as well as the PoP, before the Completer can
send:
– Comp for a CleanSharedPersist transaction
– Persist or CompPersist for a CleanSharedPersistSep transaction
• The final destination is the point at which no time is required to make the data persistent after the loss of
power. If battery failure occurs, this ensures data preservation.
See Table B2.7 for more information on response ordering guarantees.

Address, Addr

power. If battery failure occurs, this ensures data preservation.
See Table B2.7 for more information on response ordering guarantees.

Address, Addr

The Addr field specifies the address associated with the message.
A PA of 44 to 52 bits is supported. The address is carried in Addr field in the REQ and SNP channels.
The width of the Addr field is defined by the Req_Addr_Width parameter. The Addr field width is the same value
of the parameter in REQ channel, Addr[(43-51):0], and is three less than the parameter value in the SNP channel,
Addr[(43-51):3].
The Addr field is utilized in the REQ and SNP channels in the following manner:

• For Read, PrefetchTgt , Dataless, Write, and Atomic* transactions, the Addr field includes the address of the
memory location being accessed. This starts with Addr[0] mapped to bit 0 of Addr.
• For a snoop request, except SnpDVMOp, the Addr field includes the address of the location being snooped.
This starts with Addr[3] mapped to bit 0 of Addr.

a snoop request, except SnpDVMOp, the Addr field includes the address of the location being snooped.
This starts with Addr[3] mapped to bit 0 of Addr.

– Addr[(43-51):6] is the cache line address. It is sufficient to uniquely identify the cache line to be
accessed by the snoop.

. It is recommended that the snooped cache returns the data in wrap order with the critical
Identifier

The Tag field provides n sets of 4-bit tags, Tag[4*n-1:0]. Each tag is associated with a 16-byte, aligned address

Note
Prior to Issue G, the DataSource field was only applicable in SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl responses in
Non-stash type Snoop transactions. This requirement was driven by DataPull sharing the common field
location on the SNP channel. With DataPull being split out for Issue G, this restriction is removed, allowing
DataSource to be applicable in all SnpRespData or SnpRespDataPtl responses.

B13.10.62 Stream Identifier, StreamID

The StreamID field is used as a unique identifier of a stream of requests originating from one or a set of Requesters
associated with the same System MMU context.
The StreamID field is present when the DevAssign_Support property is set to Device_StreamID_SecSID1.
When interfacing to PCIe, the StreamID field is the Requester node ID associated with a single PCIe endpoint.
The StreamID field is applicable and can take any value in all device requests to the host, except for the following

The NumReq field is used in conjunction with the MultiReq field to indicate the total amount of Data associated

The least significant 3 bits of NumReq share a common field with the Size field.
When the MultiReq field is 0 The common field is considered Size .
When the MultiReq field is 1 The common field is considered NumReq.

B13.10.66 CacheLineID

The CacheLineID field indicates to which cache line a Response or Data message is related.
When applicable, the CacheLineID field must match the value of REQ. Addr[11:6] or SNP. Addr[8:3] for the
relevant line being accessed, for both multi-request and non multi-request transactions.
For example, a multi-request ReadNoSnp transaction targeting four cache lines starting at address location 0x0C0
would see CompData, or RespSepData or DataSepResp messages with the following CacheLineID values:
• 0b000011 indicating the cache line at address 0x0C0
• 0b000100 indicating the cache line at address 0x100
• 0b000101 indicating the cache line at address 0x140
• 0b000110 indicating the cache line at address 0x180
The CacheLineID field is applicable in the following Response messages, except for when they are part of a DVM
transaction:
• Comp
• RespSepData
• CompDBIDResp
• DBIDResp
• DBIDRespOrd
• ReadReceipt
• CompAck

documents.title: Channel
documents.text:
Chapter B13. Link
B13.4. Channel

ARM IHI 0050

• Permitted, but not required, to wait for forward progress on the outbound RSP channel before making
forward progress on the inbound SNP channel.
• Permitted, but not required, to wait for forward progress on the outbound DAT channel before making
forward progress on the inbound SNP channel.
• Must make forward progress on the inbound RSP channel without requiring forward progress on any other
channel.
• Must make forward progress on the inbound DAT channel without requiring forward progress on any other
channel.

Note
The requirement that a Request Node must make forward progress on the inbound RSP and DAT channel,
without requiring forward progress on any other channel, means that a Request Node must be able to accept
all Comp and CompData responses for outstanding transactions without sending any CompAck responses.

For a Subordinate Node:
• Permitted, but not required, to wait for forward progress on the outbound RSP channel before making
forward progress on the inbound REQ channel.
• When Retry_Support is:
– True: Must make forward progress on the inbound REQ channel without requiring forward progress on
the outbound DAT channel.
– RP0_Only: Must make forward progress on the inbound REQ channel for RP0 without requiring
forward progress on the outbound DAT channel. The Subordinate Node is permitted, but not required, to
wait for forward progress on the outbound DAT channel before making forward progress on the inbound
REQ channel for RP1 to RP7.
– False: Permitted, but not required, to wait for forward progress on the outbound DAT channel before
making forward progress on the inbound REQ channel.
• Must make forward progress on the inbound DAT channel without requiring forward progress on any other
channel.

documents.title: Node interface definitions
documents.text:
B13.6.1.3 RN-I
The RN-I interface uses all channels, with the exception of the SNP channel, and is used by an IO coherent
Request Node such as a GPU or IO bridge. A SNP channel is not required because an RN-I node does not include
a hardware-coherent cache or TLB.
Figure B13.7 shows the RN-I interface.

RN-I
ICN
TXREQ REQ RXREQ
RXRSP RSP TXRSP
RXDAT DAT TXDAT
TXRSP RSP RXRSP
TXDAT DAT RXDAT

Figure B13.7: RN-I interface

B13.6.2 Subordinate Nodes

This section describes the Subordinate Node interfaces:
• B13.6.2.1 SN-F and SN-I
B13.6.2.1 SN-F and SN-I
The SN-F and SN-I interfaces are identical and use a RX request channel, a TX response channel, a TX data
channel, and an RX data channel. The SN-F and SN-I receive request messages from the interconnect, and return
response messages to the interconnect. However, the SN-F and SN-I receive different types of transactions.
Figure B13.8 shows the SN-F and SN-I interface.

ICN
SN-F, SN-I
TXREQ REQ RXREQ
RXRSP RSP TXRSP
RXDAT DAT TXDAT
TXDAT DAT RXDAT

Figure B13.8: SN-F and SN-I interface

chunks.text:
B13.6.1.3 RN-I
The RN-I interface uses all channels, with the exception of the SNP channel, and is used by an IO coherent
Request Node such as a GPU or IO bridge. A SNP channel is not required because an RN-I node does not include
a hardware-coherent cache or TLB.
Figure B13.7 shows the RN-I interface.

RN-I

RXDAT DAT TXDAT
TXRSP RSP RXRSP
TXDAT DAT RXDAT

B13.6.2 Subordinate Nodes

This section describes the Subordinate Node interfaces:
• B13.6.2.1 SN-F and SN-I
B13.6.2.1 SN-F and SN-I
The SN-F and SN-I interfaces are identical and use a RX request channel, a TX response channel, a TX data
channel, and an RX data channel. The SN-F and SN-I receive request messages from the interconnect, and return
response messages to the interconnect. However, the SN-F and SN-I receive different types of transactions.
Figure B13.8 shows the SN-F and SN-I interface.

onse messages to the interconnect. However, the SN-F and SN-I receive different types of transactions.
Figure B13.8 shows the SN-F and SN-I interface.

documents.title: Increasing inter-port bandwidth
documents.text:
Chapter B13. Link
B13.7. Increasing

• All channels must be replicated even when only a subset of the channels needs increased bandwidth.
Address striping
B13.7.1.1
An optional optimization is permitted where a Request Node can specify the address striping that is used to select
between multiple interfaces. This can be specified for any number of interfaces.
A Request Node is permitted to use address striping to guide its requests to the appropriate interface without
declaring the striping algorithm being used.
A Home Node typically can filter snoops based on a snoop filter. If the snoop filter is precise, the node ID of the
Requester that cached the cache line is tracked and a snoop is sent for a subsequent request to the same cache line
on a single interface. A snoop filter that does not track precisely, or is sized to the number of components in the
system instead of the number of Request Node interfaces, is not able to isolate the single interface needed to send

Hash function example

the snoop on, unless the snoop filter knows and uses the same address striping algorithm as the Requester.
When a Request Node does not declare its striping algorithm, the snoop filters either need to be larger or the Home
must send redundant snoops. It is recommended that a Request Node that uses address striping advertises the
striping algorithm in order to be used by the Home Node.
The address striping used by a Request Node can be specified by a hash function.
B13.7.1.2
This section describes a suggested hash function to be used to distribute requests across multiple REQ interfaces.
The same hash function can be used to distribute snoops across multiple available SNP interfaces. The steps in
generating the interface number are:
1. The cache line aligned input address is first filtered through a predefined Hash Mask. The most significant
address bits that are not used must be all zeros before filtering the address. In this example, the result of the
filtering is Mask_Result.
2. The individual bits of Mask_Result are XORed to obtain the target interface.
Where the number of interfaces is a power-of-two:

B13.7.2

• For 2 interfaces:

channels on a single interface

Interfaces[0] = Mask_Result[n-1] ˆ Mask_Result[n-2] . . . Mask_Result[7] ˆ Mask_Result[6]
• For 4 interfaces:
Interfaces[1:0] = Mask_Result[n-1:n-2] ˆ Mask_Result[n-3:n-4] . . . Mask_Result[9:8] ˆ Mask_Result[7:6]
• For 8 interfaces:
Interfaces[2:0] = Mask_Result[n-1:n-3] ˆ Mask_Result[n-4:n-6] . . . Mask_Result[11:9] ˆ Mask_Result[8:6]
This example does not cover the situation where the number of interfaces is not a power-of-two.

Replicated

Rather than replicating a complete interface through more complex methods, a more efficient method to increase
the available interface bandwidth is to selectively replicate the channels that require greater bandwidth.
Figure B13.10 shows an example of replicated channels.

ARM IHI 0050

RN-F
TXREQ0 REQ0
TXREQ1 REQ1
RXRSP0 RSP0
RXRSP1 RSP1
RXDAT0 DAT0
RXDAT1 DAT1
RXSNP0 SNP0
Single interface
TXRSP0 RSP0
TXDAT0 DAT0
TXDAT1 DAT1

B13.7.2.1 Features

Figure B13.10: Replicated channels example

The main features of this method of increasing available bandwidth are described in this section.
Each channel can be selectively replicated. There are no restrictions on which channels are replicated. Typically,
replication of a channel is based on the expected bandwidth required on that channel. For example, in Figure
B13.10, TXREQ is duplicated as TXREQ0, TXREQ1, whereas RXSNP is not duplicated and has only RXSNP0.
The characteristics of the replicated channel interface are:
• All replicated DAT subchannels corresponding to a single DAT channel must be of the same width.
• The complete interface must use:
– The same NodeID
– A single TxnID pool
• Messages within a transaction can use any subchannel:
– A response message does not need to use the same subchannel as the request. For example, Request on

SACTIVE of , LINKACTIVE and SYSCOREQ/SYSCOACK signals, and

TXREQ0 can give a response on either RXRSP0 or RXRSP1.
– Multiple response messages for a single request can come on any subchannel. For example, DBIDResp
for a write transaction is received on RXRSP0 whereas the corresponding Comp can be received on
RXRSP1.
• Like non-replicated channels, replicated channels do not provide any in-channel ordering guarantees.
• All link crediting is done on a subchannel basis.
– Cannot use the credit for TXREQ0 to send the flit on TXREQ1.
– Credits are required to be provided by the Receiver on all subchannels.
• Protocol credits are for the combined TXREQ channel.
• There is no support for powering down a subchannel individually.
• The two parts of a DVM snoop can come on either subchannel. Each part can be on a different subchannels.
• The number of subchannels on two connected interfaces must match.
• There must be only one set
optional broadcast control pins.
• Interface property CCF_Wrap_Order is not permitted to be set to True when the interface includes replicated
DAT channels.

Note
When a channel has been replicated on an interface, IMPLEMENTATION it is
SPECIFIC which of the
subchannels is used for a transfer.
